channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: A Viral Knicks Moment, Brought to You by a Prediction Market
item.description: What do a Jalen Brunson look-alike contest, a buzzy catchphrase and a dancing robot outside Madison Square Garden have in common?
item.dc:creator: Troy Closson, Santul Nerkar and Lauren McCarthy
item.media:description: Jake Epstein, a content creator, hosted a Jalen Brunson look-alike contest for Polymarket, a prediction market company, on Wednesday in Washington Square Park.

item.title: Have You Bought a Tesla Recently? We Want to Hear From You.
item.description: Tesla sales are rebounding in Europe even though many people have negative feelings about Elon Musk, the company’s chief executive. We want to know why.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:description: Tesla’s car sales took a big hit in Europe last year, in part because many people were turned off by the political views of the company’s chief executive, Elon Musk.

item.title: The Washington Outlet NOTUS Won’t Be ‘The Star’ After All
item.description: The publication is abandoning a planned rebranding after resolving a trademark dispute with the new publisher of The Washington Star.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:description: Readers of The Washington Star-News, a previous name of The Washington Star, in 1974.

item.title: Are You a Recent College Graduate Who Moved Back Home? We Want to Hear From You.
item.description: If you lived with your parents for nine months or more after graduation, please tell us how you came to the decision and whether the job market influenced your thinking.
item.dc:creator: Noam Scheiber
item.media:credit: David L. Ryan/The Boston Globe, via Getty Images

item.title: Federal Energy Regulator Seeks to Limit Conflicts Over Data Centers
item.description: The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission directed grid managers to make changes that protect individuals from higher electricity bills while giving data centers access to power faster.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.media:credit: Dustin Chambers for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Meta data center in Georgia. U.S. technology companies in recent years have been building dozens of power-hungry data centers to develop artificial intelligence.

item.title: Penske Media Is Buying What’s Left of Vox Media
item.description: The deal will put online publications like Popsugar and Eater under the same roof as Rolling Stone and Variety.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Matt Winkelmeyer/GA, via The Hollywood Reporter, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The Vox Media sites are latest acquisition by Jay Penske, the youngest son of the automotive tycoon Roger Penske.

item.title: Education Department Lowers Student Loan Interest Rates for Two Years
item.description: The department, citing high defaults, is reducing interest rates by up to one percentage point for two years if borrowers pay automatically.
item.dc:creator: Ron Lieber, Tara Siegel Bernard and Michael C. Bender
item.media:description: The temporary rate reduction starts July 1, and borrowers must enroll in automatic payment to obtain it.

item.title: Warsh Wants the Fed to Send Fewer Signals. That Comes With Risks.
item.description: Investors piled on bets for higher borrowing costs after Kevin Warsh opted against providing policy guidance at his first meeting as Federal Reserve chairman.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh, the Federal Reserve chairman, believes central bank officials should be more guarded in what they share.

item.title: Warsh’s Hawkish Turn Has Scrambled the Math on Rates
item.description: The futures market now sees at least one interest rate increase this year, as the Federal Reserve’s new leader, Kevin Warsh, vows to fight inflation.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Vivienne Walt
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin Warsh, the Fed chairman, surprised many on Wall Street with hawkish statements about the need to combat inflation.

item.title: Inflation Will Linger Despite U.S.-Iran Deal, British Officials Warn
item.description: Bank of England policymakers kept interest rates on hold, saying the impact of the war in Iran was still a major source of uncertainty for the economy.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Policymakers at the Bank of England have held rates steady since late last year, as the war in the Middle East pushed up energy prices.

item.title: As India Temporarily Bans Telegram, Which Other Countries Have Restricted the Messaging App?
item.description: Several countries have restricted the messaging app, a valuable platform for dissidents, criminals and extremists. Here’s an overview.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:credit: Sasha Maslov for The New York Times

item.title: The Small Investors Behind SpaceX’s Stock Rally
item.description: The company’s blockbuster trading debut is the latest example of how individual investors have become a force in financial markets.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni and Joe Rennison
item.media:credit: Andres Kudacki for The New York Times
item.media:description: Since its stock market debut last Friday, SpaceX has been the most bought stock by retail investors each day.

item.title: All Aboard! Brands Jump on the Knicks Bandwagon.
item.description: Companies — including some with no ties to New York or basketball — have posted memes, changed their avatars to Knicks blue and orange, and cheered the team in their captions.
item.dc:creator: Tania Ganguli

item.title: Why Flight Prices Might Not Fall After the U.S.-Iran Deal
item.description: Jet fuel may stay expensive for months, and airlines know travelers are willing to pay more for tickets.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Jet fuel prices nearly doubled after the war with Iran started, and they are unlikely to fall substantially for months, analysts said.

item.title: What to Know Before Buying SpaceX Stock, According to Financial Advisers
item.description: SpaceX has rapidly become one of the world’s most valuable companies. Even so, experts say investing in single stocks can be risky.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni

item.title: They Booked an Airbnb Ahead of the World Cup. Can a Host Demand They Pay More?
item.description: A family booked early for a big soccer trip to Southern California, then a new host suddenly raised the price. Why did Airbnb leave them in the lurch?
item.dc:creator: Seth Kugel

item.title: Average U.S. Gas Price Falls Below $4 After U.S.-Iran Agreement
item.description: Drivers are enjoying some relief at the pump after the United States and Iran signed an agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Gasoline prices rose sharply after the start of U.S.-led war with Iran.

item.title: Oil Prices Fall as U.S.-Iran Deal to Reopen Hormuz Takes Effect
item.description: Oil prices fell and stocks rose after Pakistan announced that the agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz would take effect immediately.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Warsh Makes His Case With Jargon, and a Penchant for Detail
item.description: The Federal Reserve’s new chairman held a press conference where he bantered with reporters and laid out a vision for change at the central bank.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh, the Federal Reserve chairman, after the central bank’s meeting in Washington on Wednesday.

item.title: Fed holds rates steady at Warsh’s first meeting.
item.description: Officials at the Federal Reserve were split between no cuts this year and one or more rate increases as they braced for higher inflation, according to a new set of projections.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith

item.title: Takeaways from Kevin Warsh’s first meeting as Fed chairman.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith

item.title: See How the First Fed Statement Under Warsh Evolved
item.description: Interest rates didn’t change, but the language in Fed’s policy statement, which it released alongside its rate decision today, certainly did.
item.dc:creator: Christine Zhang

item.title: Elon Musk’s Next Move May Be a Mega-Merger of SpaceX and Tesla
item.description: Some shareholders might object, but there is little they could do, legal experts say.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing and Ryan Mac
item.media:description: Tesla has sold car and batteries to SpaceX, and the two companies are jointly working on chips and software.

item.title: How to read the Fed’s projections like a pro.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith

item.title: Political Spotlight Shines on Warsh for His First Fed Meeting as Chairman
item.description: The new Fed chairman is bound to face the unyielding attention of the president that nominated him.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm

item.title: Who is Kevin Warsh, the Fed’s new chairman?
item.description: Mr. Warsh will make his public debut as the Federal Reserve’s chairman on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember and Ben Casselman

item.title: Warsh is focused on the Fed’s balance sheet. Here’s what you need to know.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith

item.title: How the Fed’s rate decisions affect your wallet.
item.dc:creator: Tara Siegel Bernard

item.title: Allowing Iran to Charge Fees in Strait of Hormuz Would Set ‘Dangerous Precedent,’ Maersk CEO Says
item.description: With the United States and Iran nearing a deal to reopen the Persian Gulf, the shipping giant is treading cautiously.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Charlotte de la Fuente for The New York Times
item.media:description: “There’s been a couple of times where it was declared open and was not really open,” said Vincent Clerc, the chief executive of Maersk.

item.title: Stocks Drop on Fed Decision, and Oil Is Muted Amid Peace Deal Talks
item.description: Traders are waiting for U.S. and Iranian officials to meet in Switzerland on Friday, when they are expected to sign an initial agreement and begin a 60-day cease-fire.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: SpaceX’s Growing Warchest
item.description: Shares in Elon Musk’s SpaceX have been on a tear, giving the rockets and artificial intelligence company financial resources to box out competitors.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: The SpaceX rally looks set to continue again on Wednesday.

item.title: Prediction Markets’ Next Major Bet: Wall St. Traders
item.description: Platforms like Kalshi let millions wager on sports and pop culture. They’re now hoping institutions will use them for big-ticket trading like hedging.
item.dc:creator: Michael J. de la Merced
item.media:credit: Marshall Scheuttle for The New York Times
item.media:description: Prediction markets like Kalshi are known for letting bettors wager on politics, sports and more.

item.title: Japan Raids Ice Cream Giants in Cartel Investigation
item.description: The Fair Trade Commission said that it suspected six leading ice cream manufacturers fixed the prices of popular frozen treats.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández and Hisako Ueno
item.media:description: Sales of ice creams, commonly sold at convenience stores in Japan, are increasing as summer approaches.

item.title: Fed Meeting Live Updates: Kevin Warsh to Address U.S. Economy as Fed Holds Interest Rates Steady
item.description: Officials at the Federal Reserve were split between no cuts this year and one or more rate increases as they braced for higher inflation, according to a new set of projections.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith

item.title: Rachel Reeves, UK Chancellor, Confronts the Political Challenges of Fiscal Stability
item.description: Rachel Reeves came into office promising to adhere to strict rules on spending and borrowing. Two years later, a special election could determine if she will continue in her role.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rachel Reeves, chancellor of the Exchequer, argues that fiscal stability is essential as Britain confronts rising costs and public debt.

item.title: The U.S. Economy Is Leaving Small Businesses Behind
item.description: Small businesses say relentless pressures from tariffs and higher energy prices have sapped their resilience and finances.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember
item.media:credit: Kaiti Sullivan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kirsten Davenport-Norwood, who owns Greene Thumb Landscape in Indianapolis

item.title: ‘Backrooms’ and ‘Obsession’ Disrupt the 2026 Summer Box Office
item.description: “Backrooms” and “Obsession,” two breakout horror films from first-time directors in their 20s, have arguably redefined what a summer blockbuster can be.
item.dc:creator: Keith Collins
item.category: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu (Movie)

item.title: Who Will Get Spirit Airlines’ Coveted Flights at LaGuardia?
item.description: A bankruptcy court has started the process to sell the airline’s right to fly to and from the New York City airport, but finding a buyer could be hard.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.category: Port Authority of New York and New Jersey
item.media:credit: DeSean McClinton-Holland for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Spirit Airlines guest services counter at LaGuardia Airport.

item.title: The Cloud Has Sound: The Unrelenting and Unseen Cost of A.I. Data Centers
item.description: As tech giants rush to build infrastructure, some residents who live near data centers say a constant low-frequency vibration is ruining their health and homes.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: Danielle Parhizkaran/The Boston Globe, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A 350,000-square-foot data center in Lowell, Mass., is abutted by homes. The site was once the Lowell Bleachery and Dye Works, and then for six decades the Prince Spaghetti factory.

item.title: A New Era of Super-Hybrids to Ease Range Anxiety
item.description: Extended-range electric vehicles promise to reduce costs and appeal to America’s appetite for adventure. And they’re coming to showrooms soon.
item.dc:creator: Tom Voelk
item.media:description: The technology that powers extended-range electric vehicles, like the 2026 Jeep Grand Wagoneer, solves the towing issues common among regular electric vehicles.

item.title: Beijing’s New Message to Its Citizens: Your Money Belongs at Home
item.description: Eager to keep capital within its borders, China is restricting the ways individuals can engage with global markets.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan

item.title: SpaceX Is More Valuable Than Meta, Broadcom and Many Others
item.description: In its first few days of trading, Elon Musk’s newly listed rocket maker has become one of the world’s most valuable companies.
item.dc:creator: Jason Karaian

item.title: Best-Selling Memoirist Sues Classmate Who Said She Used Her Story
item.description: Amy Griffin contended that she was defamed when a former classmate accused her in a lawsuit of appropriating parts of her story of being sexually abused for “The Tell.”
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang and Ali Watkins
item.media:description: Amy Griffin discussing her book “The Tell” with the actress Mariska Hargitay in New York City last year.

item.title: Pizza Hut Sold to Two Firms for $2.7 Billion
item.description: Yum China will acquire Pizza Hut’s locations in mainland China. The private equity firm LongRange Capital will buy its locations in the United States and elsewhere.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell
item.media:description: A Pizza Hut restaurant in San Pablo, Calif. Pizza Hut at one point was the largest pizza chain in the country.

item.title: War Hangs Over American Farmers as Fertilizer Prices Rise
item.description: The Iran war and lower crop prices have hurt makers of sulfur-based supplements. But farmers won’t skip nitrogen-based fertilizers, no matter the cost.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Draper
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tim Gruber for The New York Times
item.media:description: Farmers of big row crops like corn consider the use of nitrogen fertilizer to be nonnegotiable.

item.title: Call It a ‘Book-cation’ or a ‘Readaway,’ Literary Travel Is Having a Moment
item.description: Resort book clubs, tour companies, hotel libraries and a growing number of literary festivals are offering readers new ways to indulge their interests.
item.dc:creator: Elaine Glusac

item.title: Working Parents Say They’re in a Losing Battle Over Their Time
item.description: Most like having a job but say it leaves them too few hours for their children and other pursuits, a Pew survey shows.
item.dc:creator: Claire Cain Miller
item.media:credit: Natalie Keyssar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Most parents say they want to both work and parent. But they need flexibility.

item.title: European Union Lawmakers Approve Much-Delayed Trade Deal With U.S.
item.description: After nearly a year of wrangling, the deal the European Union struck with President Trump in Turnberry, Scotland, is headed for final approval.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek
item.media:description: The European Parliament voted on a trade deal between the United States and the European Union in Strasbourg, France, on Tuesday.

item.title: Riding High After I.P.O., SpaceX Will Buy A.I. Start-Up for $60 Billion
item.description: SpaceX exercised its option to acquire Cursor in an all-stock deal, bolstering Elon Musk’s ambitions in artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac and Gregory Schmidt
item.media:credit: Jordan Vonderhaar for The New York Times
item.media:description: The deal to acquire Cursor signals how Elon Musk is delving further into A.I. and expanding SpaceX from its main businesses of building rocket ships and offering satellite internet service.

item.title: Big Questions for Warsh as New Era for the Fed Begins
item.description: Interest rates, inflation and independence are among the challenges the new chairman of the Federal Reserve faces as the central bank’s policymakers meet.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Kevin Warsh is set to address reporters tomorrow in his first news conference as Fed chairman.
